--- SOURCE 1: Students' Perception of Cyber Threat Severity ---
URL: https://kth.diva-portal.org/smash/get/diva2:1793706/FULLTEXT01.pdf

SUMMARY:
This study aims to investigate the alignment between students' perception of cy- ber threats and their actual risk levels.

FULL CONTENT:
Degree Project in Computer Science and Engineering Second cycle, 30 credits Students’ Perception of Cyber Threat Severity Investigating Alignment with Actual Risk Levels RAMTIN ERFANI TORBAGHANI Stockholm, Sweden 2023 Abstract This study aims to investigate the alignment between students’ perception of cy-ber threats and their actual risk levels. A mixed-method approach was used, where data was collected from Swedish university students through questionnaires, cap-turing their perception, familiarity, experience, and protective behaviors. Informa-tion regarding the actual risk levels of cyber attacks was obtained from interviews with cyber security professionals and other expert sources, such as cyber security reports. The results showed that students perceive malware, ransomware, phishing, and insecure passwords as the most dangerous threats to society, while denial of ser-vice (DoS) attacks and packet sniffing were considered less severe. These findings align somewhat with the suggested threat levels. However, notable proportions of students perceived these threats as moderately dangerous or less severe, suggesting room for improvement in their understanding. The results also showed that pro-tective behaviors among students are generally low, particularly in regards to IoT security. Future work should therefore explore the public’s perception, protective behavior and knowledge of IoT security, but also attacks that are common against such devices.
Keywords Cyber Security, Cyber Threat, Perception, Cyber Attack, Ransomware, Malware, Phishing Packet Sniffing, Denial of Service, Insecure Passwords IoT, Vulnerability Scanning, Brute Force Attacks Awareness, Behaviour, Familiarity, University Students 1 Sammanfattning Denna studie jämför universitetsstudenters uppfattning om hur farliga olika cyber-hot är med de faktiska risknivåerna för dessa hot. Data på studenternas uppfattning, bekantskap, erfarenhet och beteenden samlades in genom frågeformulär, medans information om cyberhotens faktiska risknivåer inhämtades från intervjuer med cy-bersäkerhetsproffs och andra experskällor som cybersäkerhetsrapporter och artik-lar. Resultaten visade att studenterna uppfattar malware, ransomware, phishing och osäkra lösenord som de farligaste hoten mot samhället, medan denial of ser-vice (DoS)-attacker och packet sniffing ansågs vara mindre allvarliga. Dessa fynd överensstämde något med de föreslagna risknivåerna. Dock ansåg en anmärkn-ingsvärd andel av studenterna dessa hot som måttligt farliga eller mindre allvarliga, vilket tyder på utrymme för förbättringar i deras förståelse. Resultaten visade också att skyddande beteenden bland studenter generellt är låga, särskilt när det gäller IoT-säkerhet. Framtida studier bör därför utforska allmänhetens uppfattning, sky-ddsbeteende och kunskap om IoT-säkerhet, men även attacker som är vanliga mot sådana enheter.

Important sectors of society such as energy, transport, finance, healthcare are becoming increasingly dependent on digital technology to be able to conduct their core business. But dig-italisation also means that the economy and society are exposed to cyber threats [21].
Across Europe, cybercrime and the number of cyberattacks are increasing. This type of crime is also becoming increasingly sophisticated. The trend is likely to grow stronger in the future, given that 22.3 billion IoT devices worldwide will be con-nected by 2024 [21].
As the number of cyber attacks increases, the EU chooses to prioritize and invest more resources in cyber security. In May 2020, the EU allocated 49 million euro to promote innovation in cybersecurity and information protection systems. [21] The EU has also committed to invest approximately 7.6 billion euro on the Digital Europe Programme. The program will run from 2021 - 2027 and will spend around one fifth of its budget on cybersecurity and trust [21] [22].
The European Cybersecurity Month (ECSM) is a campaign coordinated by the Eu-ropean Commission and the European Union Agency for CyberSecurity (ENISA). Ev-ery year, ESCM holds a number of activities across Europe, including workshops, webinars, trainings, presentations, conferences and more throughout the month of October. The aim of the campaign is to raise awareness of cybersecurity among EU citizens and organizations [20]. In Sweden, the cybersecurity month takes place un-der the name Think safe and is run by The Swedish Civil Contingencies Agency (MSB) and The Swedish Police Authority (Polismyndigheten) [50].
In Sweden, a vast majority use the internet daily. In 2021 it was estimated that 95 percent of the Swedish population have access to the internet, and that 9 out of 10 are online daily [28]. In a yearly survey by The Swedish Civil Contingencies Agency (MSB) it was shown that 1 out of 5 Swedes had been subjected to some type of IT-related attack [51]. As technical security improves, attacks that take advantage of the human factor, e.g. bad practice or lack of knowledge, become easier for attackers to carry out. This gives rise to questions related to the interplay between different factors that affect information security in socio-technical systems [24].
A reasonable consequence of the EU’s efforts to educate the public and the in-creasing number of cyber threats taking place around the world is that private indi-viduals are becoming more aware of the potential risks that their online actions may expose them to. But public opinion about what is safe and what is dangerous does not always align with the actual truth. Some cyber threats can be perceived as harm-less, when in fact they are devastating. Other threats can be perceived as dangerous, while in fact they can be easily circumvented. It is therefore interesting to examine how well the public’s notions of cyber threats are in line with state-of-the-art knowl-edge of how dangerous the threats actually are.
Gaining information on how citizens perceive digital risks, their attitudes and levels of knowledge can provide a deeper understanding of the type of information the target group needs to improve their skills and knowledge of cybersecurity.
7 1.2 Aim and Research Question This study aims to shed light on what the public understands about cyber threats and how they prioritize different choices around security. A deeper understanding of this can help us understand how security should be improved.
To delimit the purpose the following research questions were specified: • How does students’ perception of various cyber threats align with the state-of-the-art knowledge about the actual severity of these threats?
• Is there any relationship between students perception, familiarity, protective behaviours and knowledge of threats?
1.3 Previous Research The field of cyber security keeps expanding and becoming more crucial, covering numerous research topics. A key research focus within the field of cyber security in-volves the enhancement of cyber security awareness, usually by identifying factors that contribute to reaching this goal. This part will outline previous related work on cyber security related to knowledge, behavior and perception, mainly in the educa-tional sphere.
Kovaˇ cevi´ c et al. conducted a study using a questionnaire-based survey with participants drawn from the student population at the Faculty of Security Stud-ies, University of Belgrade, Serbia. The aim of their research was to thoroughly analyze cyber security awareness and explore the potential impact of various factors on cyber security behavior. These factors include socio-demographic variables, previous experiences with threats, cyber security perceptions, IT us-age and knowledge. Their findings revealed that the effects of knowledge, ex-periences and perception emerged as stronger factors than the effect of socio-demographics in influencing cyber security behavior. Furthermore, despite the students being digital natives, they did not feel secure within the cyberspace, showed insecure behavior, and lacked adequate knowledge to protect them-selves against cyber threats [32].
Kenneth Olmstead and Aaron Smith carried out a structured interview re-search of adults in the United States to investigate their cybersecurity habits and attitudes. Their report aimed to understand the experiences, perception and trust levels of Americans concerning data theft, fraud, and personal in-formation security, as well as their expectations regarding future cyberattacks.
The results indicated that a considerable share of Americans (64%) had ex-perienced a significant data breach, and a large portion of the public lacked trust in key institutions, particularly the federal government and social me-dia platforms, to keep their personal data safe. A majority of the respondents also expect major cyberattacks on public infrastructure (70%) or banking and financial systems (66%) within the next five years. Despite their experiences and concerns, most Americans did not express significant worries about cy-bersecurity in their personal lives or expectations for public institutions. Ad-ditionally, a considerable share of the public admitted to not consistently in-corporating cybersecurity best practices into their digital lives, with only 12% 8 using password management software, even though it is considered the safest and most secure method for tracking and maintaining online passwords [54].
Anwar et al. carried out a cross-sectional survey study among employees from diverse organizations and used structural equation modeling to assess gen-der’s effect as a moderator variable in the relationship between psychosocial factors and self-reported cybersecurity behaviors. The findings revealed that female participants reported significantly lower security self-efficacy, lower levels of computer skills and less prior experience with computer security than the male participants. The researchers also emphasize that these differences might have resulted from overconfidence among male participants or under-confidence among female participants in their self-assessment [2].
Cain et al. conducted a survey study to evaluate end-users understanding of cybersecurity concepts, threat knowledge, and cyber hygiene behaviors. The participants’ prior training and experiences in cybersecurity were also inves-tigated. The sample, recruited via Mechanical Turk, consisted of participants divided equally among ages 18 to 55+, with 92% of them being from the United States. The study revealed that males possessed greater cyber hygiene knowl-edge compared to females. But despite this difference in knowledge, there was no significant difference in cyber security behavior between males and females. The authors also reported that no significant correlation could be identified between participants’ past experiences with cyber attacks and their current cyber hygiene habits. Finally, it was found that although a majority, 81%, of the respondents had some prior training in cyber security, they did not score highly in regards to cyber hygiene behavior or knowledge [16].
Moallem conducted survey research on cybersecurity awareness among uni-versity students in the San Francisco Bay Area of California, USA. A region known for having one of the world’s most advanced communities in terms of technology, development, wealth and population diversity. The findings showed that although university students think they are being observed on-line, they still lack acute awareness of how to protect their data. They report low levels of 2FA usage, password complexity for accounts, privacy settings, and protection against social engineering. Moallem also identified that ed-ucational institutions do not actively improve awareness among university students. Furthermore, it was stated that this improvement in awareness is important for increasing students’ knowledge and teaching them defensive measures against potential cyber-attacks [45].
McCormac et al. carried out a study to explore the relationship between indi-viduals’ Information Security Awareness (ISA) and individual difference vari-ables, such as gender, personality, age and risk-taking tendencies. The Hu-man Aspects of Information Security Questionnaire (HAIS-Q) was employed to measure the ISA of working Australians. Their findings showed that emo-tional stability, agreeableness, conscientiousness, and risk-taking tendencies significantly contributed to the variance in individuals’ ISA. However, age and gender did not show any impact on information security awareness [44].
9 Jeske and van Schaik carried out an investigation assessing university stu-dents’ familiarity with various cyber threats. This process was executed through an online survey in which participants were presented with descriptions of 16 different threats and asked to indicate their familiarity with each. The authors identified three groupings based on the responses: those more familiar with new threats, those more familiar with well-recognized threats, and those fa-miliar about all threats (both new and established). The study found that the amount of Internet experience and time spent online were predictors of fa-miliarity, and that both factors also are indirect predictors of cyber security hygiene [30].
Gratian et al. conducted a survey examining the relationship between human attributes, such as personality traits, demographics, decision-making styles and risk-taking preferences affect cybersecurity behavior intentions among staff and students at a public university. Their findings indicated that gender, personality traits, decision-making and financial risk-taking serve as reliable predictors of good security behaviors. Gender emerged as a significant pre-dictor of password creation behavior. Notably, females tended to use weaker password generation practices than males. Since their results presented both consistencies and contradictions with prior research, the authors emphasize that the specific environment matters when investigating a human popula-tion with respect to cyber security awareness [26].
Kruger et al. carried out a survey study aimed at assessing the feasibility of an information security vocabulary test to evaluate awareness levels. Their questionnaire had two parts, the first part tested the comprehension of terms, and the second part assessed the behavior of participants’, who were univer-sity students. The findings of the study demonstrated the benefits of using a vocabulary test to assess security awareness levels, as a significant correlation between knowledge of concepts and behavior was observed [33].
Al-Janabi and Al-Shourbaji conducted a questionnaire-based survey research to evaluate cyber security awareness among undergraduate students, researchers and academic staff within educational environments in the Middle East. Their research aims to provide insight into the risks and consequences related to the identified level of awareness. The study revealed that participants lacked the necessary comprehension and awareness of cyber security concepts. The au-thors also highlighted potential consequences based on the weaknesses found in their survey, and proposed recommendations to address these [29].
Emily A. Vogels and Monica Anderson conducted a study using self-administered web surveys to assess the digital knowledge of American adults. Their sur-vey aimed to evaluate their understanding of various digital topics, including cybersecurity and the business aspects of social media companies. Their re-sult showed that adults possessing a bachelor’s or advanced degree, as well as those under the age of 50, tend to achieve higher scores on digital knowl-edge questions. Furthermore, it was observed that younger adults generally outperform older adults on digital knowledge questions [1].
10 These studies consistently identify multiple factors influencing cybersecurity aware-ness. The identified factors include behavior, knowledge [59] [33] [29] [45] [16] [1] [32], perceptions [30] [45] [54] [32] and socio-demographic variables such as gender and age [26] [44] [2] [16] [54] [1] [32].
Despite there being findings indicating the influence of various factors on cy-bersecurity awareness, notable contradictions exist in how they impact awareness.
These contradictions primarily emerge in relation to perception and socio-demographic factors. This observation aligns with the notion that the cybersecurity awareness levels of a population depends on the environment it was sampled from, as sug-gested in the study by Gratien et al [26].
Another common theme among the studies mentioned above is specific types of cyber threats that questions and assessments were centered around. These threats include Phishing [45] [29] [33] [1] [16] [26] [30] [44] [59], Denial of Service (DoS) [29], Malware [29] [30] [2], Ransomware [45] [30], attacks exploiting password-related vul-nerabilities [54] [45] [29] [33] [1] [16] [2] [59] and attacks exploiting weak network connections, such as insecure encryption protocols and public Wi-Fi [29] [54] [1] [45].
Previous research has primarily employed quantitative methods, such as struc-tured interviews, questionnaires and surveys [59] [33] [29] [30] [26] [45] [44] [2] [16] [54] [1], to evaluate awareness of cyber threats. Although these studies have been informative, there is a need for more qualitative research or mixed-method research that combines both quantitative and qualitative methods. In addition, there are not many studies that have investigated the public’s perception of cyber-threat severity and whether perception matches how dangerous these threats actually are.
1.4 Research Design and Scope The research design of this study aims to investigate the alignment between stu-dents’ perception of cyber threats and the actual risk levels posed by these attacks.
To achieve this objective, the study employs a mixed-method approach, comprising both quantitative and qualitative data collection techniques.
Quantitative data is collected through a questionnaire distributed to students currently enrolled in Swedish universities. The questionnaire comprises closed ques-tions that seek to understand if the participants protect themselves against specific cyber threats, how they do so, and their perception of the danger associated with these attacks. The cyber threats included in the questionnaire will mainly be those identified in section 1.3 Previous Research. These threats include, but are not lim-ited to, Ransomware, Malware, DoS, Phishing password-related attacks, attacks that exploit weak network connections and attacks against critical societal functions.
Qualitative data is derived from interviews conducted with cyber security pro-fessionals who hold junior and senior positions in security firms, universities, and authorities such as the police. This data will be used to determine the actual risk lev-els associated with the cyber threats mentioned earlier. Other expert sources, such as reports and articles from Swedish authorities will be used to further support the findings of the qualitative data.
Although it is interesting to study the public perception of cyber threats with respect to sociodemographic variables (as mentioned in section 1.3 Previous Re-search), this study will not use bivariate or multivariate analysis techniques. This de-cision is primarily due to the limited amount of time of the study. Instead, the anal-ysis of questionnaire data will be conducted using univariate analysis techniques.
11 The limitations associated with solely relying on univariate analysis are discussed in section 3.4 Limitations.
12 2 Theoretical Background Central concepts for the study will be explained in this section. The first half will cover different threats, and the second half will cover the different methods used to analyze the questionnaire and interview data.
2.1 Internet of Things - Security and Risks The Internet of Things (IoT) encompasses a variety of different devices for private and industrial use, e.g. smart refrigerators, thermostats and TVs. However, the secu-rity of IoT devices are often weak, leaving them vulnerable to attackers seeking entry into home or corporate networks [47] [23] [41].
Several factors contribute to the challenges associated with IoT security.
• The rapid growth of IoT devices. The number of IoT devices is expected to grow rapidly, and the communication paths between them are predicted to expand even more quickly. Moreover, the number of variants of hardware, software, and protocols will increase, leading to challenges in comprehending the necessary measures to secure these systems [48] [23] [41].
• Lack of security features in many IoT devices. Vendors may rush to intro-duce new smart devices to the market, or face difficulties in developing se-curity interfaces. As a result, devices often lack a comprehensive IoT security framework, leaving them vulnerable [23].
• Improper setup and maintenance of IoT devices. Users frequently fail to set up and maintain IoT devices correctly. Neglect of security configurations and privacy considerations, ultimately leaves devices exposed to potential attacks [23].
The security of an IoT device is therefore dependent on its implementation, but also how it is installed, maintained, and used.
2.1.1 IoT Risks IoT risks can be categorized based on the adverse effects they pose on confidential-ity, integrity, and accessibility [48].
• Confidentiality. IoT devices can be used as stepping stones into IT systems to steal information. Since these devices, e.g. smart microphones and cameras, in many cases are intended to collect information, they enable the gathering of individual information and espionage [48] [23].
• Integrity, where IoT is the target. IoT devices, that lack security features, are vulnerable to unauthorized access. These devices can be targeted by attackers for malware infections, posing threats at both individual and societal levels.
For instance, tampering with an individual’s medical devices, or widespread manipulation of smart grids [48] [23].
• Integrity, where IoT is the tool. The potential to hijack IoT devices, and in-fecting these with malware, enables the creation of botnets. Attackers can use these botnets to hijack more IoT devices, or to carry out DDoS attacks [48] [23] [41].
• Availability. IoT devices may become inoperable due to extortion methods like ransomware or involvement in botnets [48].
13 2.1.2 Strategies to Improve IoT Security It is advised to ensure that devices meet essential security requirements. Below are some example requirements that consumers should expect: • The device has built-in security attributes. The device has built-in security attributes that support e.g. encryption and anonymity [49].
• The software is maintained and updated. The software used is continuously maintained and known security flaws have been fixed [49].
• Support for secure downloads of updates. Updates, e.g. security updates, can be downloaded in a secure way [49].
• Support for strong password protection. The devices have good password protection, for example, multi-factor authentication, or default login creden-tials that are unique for each device [49].
• Security is considered throughout the development process. The security aspect is taken into account in all stages of the development process, includ-ing the choice of platforms, programming languages and tools [49].
2.2 Phishing Phishing is one of the most common methods attackers use to spread malware and obtain login credentials, as well as bank and credit card information. This technique involves the attacker sending a message designed to deceive the victim into clicking a link, downloading an attachment, providing requested information, or even com-pleting a payment [11] [5].
Attackers often send phishing messages to numerous recipients, hoping that someone will become a victim. However, some attackers may also adopt a targeted approach, as seen in spear phishing [11] [43].
Some example outcomes of phishing attacks include identity theft, financial theft, corporate espionage, and data theft. Additionally, phishing attacks serve as an effec-tive means of propagating malware, such as ransomware. For instance, when vic-tims open email attachments, they may unintentionally download malicious code and software onto their computers [5] [11].
2.2.1 Spear Phishing Spear phishing is a targeted form of phishing attack that focuses on specific individ-uals or organizations, often with customized content that is specifically designed for the targeted individuals or organizations [5] [43].
2.2.2 Phishing vectors Some of the most common categories of phishing vectors include: • Email phishing: The most common phishing type is email phishing, where attackers use emails to distribute malicious links or attachments containing malware [5] [43].
• Vishing: Vishing involves scamming people over the phone. Automated calls, such as robocalls, are commonly used by vishing attackers [5] [43].
14 • Smishing: Smishing is the process of tricking people via SMS [5] [43].
• Social media phishing: Hacked social media accounts are sometimes used by attackers to propagate phishing messages. These messages are usually sent to the hacked users’ friends and contacts. Attackers could also create accounts and phish others using these fake profiles [5].
2.2.3 Defensive Measures Against Phishing The following section outlines several countermeasures that can be employed to prevent or defend against phishing attacks.
• Awareness and education: Stay informed and updated on the latest types of phishing scams to stay ahead of the threat [5].
• Be skeptical and cautious: To prevent falling prey to phishing attacks, it is advised to exercise caution when handling suspicious emails. This involves refraining from opening emails from unfamiliar senders and being cautious when clicking on links or downloading attachments. Additionally, one should also avoid responding to messages that require personal information, urge im-mediate action, or contain suspicious requests [5] [11] [61] [43].

ABSTRACT
--------

Why do cyberattacks elevate perceptions of threat even as they fail to generate truly destructive effects? We argue that people employ motivated reasoning to align cyberattacks with their preconceptions. Fielding a two-wave panel survey immediately before and after a cyberattack on an Israeli hospital, we confirm that motivated reasoning plays a crucial role in how people interpret cyber events. Results show that people already predisposed to perceive cyberattacks as threatening experience a significant increase in perceptions of threat, while those initially viewing cyberattacks as harmless also have their beliefs reinforced. The data reveals how even minor incidents bolster cyber doom convictions.

Introduction
------------

Cyberattacks are today as common as the internet itself (Bada & Nurse, [Citation 2020](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/19331681.2024.2420669#)). In recent years, these digital threats have begun to threaten critical infrastructure, which is made vulnerable by a culture of hyperconnectivity and our mounting reliance on digital systems. Hospitals, power grids, and transportation networks have all regularly come under attack (Kimani et al., [Citation 2019](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/19331681.2024.2420669#); Lemay & Leblanc, [Citation 2018](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/19331681.2024.2420669#); Sullivan et al., [Citation 2023](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/19331681.2024.2420669#)), and even if the attacks have yet to cause mass casualties, they generate intense fear among the public (Lawson & Middleton, [Citation 2019](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/19331681.2024.2420669#)). Indeed, such is the public panic surrounding cyber threats, that national polls consistently find that fears of cyberterrorism are ranked as the foremost security threat facing societies – higher than pandemics, nuclear weapons, or war (Brenan, [Citation 2021](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/19331681.2024.2420669#)). Over time, people’s fears of cyber threats have begun to take on a life of their own, spawning exaggerated notions of impending and unstoppable cyber-Armageddon (Gomez & Whyte, [Citation 2021](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/19331681.2024.2420669#)).

It remains an oddity that people persist in holding such exaggerated beliefs about deadly, lurking cyber threats when the evidence suggests that most cyberattacks are characterized by a relatively limited scope and constitute more of an irritant than an apocalyptic danger (Aleroud et al., [Citation 2022](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/19331681.2024.2420669#)). However, perhaps the durability of threatening cyber narratives can be explained by the way in which media (Jarvis et al., [Citation 2017](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/19331681.2024.2420669#)) and elites (Schneider, [Citation 2017](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/19331681.2024.2420669#)) depict cyberattacks, and how these narratives serve to contextualize a given incident. In an environment of deep uncertainty and technological complexity cyberattack (Gomez & Shandler, [Citation 2024](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/19331681.2024.2420669#); Kaminska, [Citation 2021](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/19331681.2024.2420669#)), people are forced to make assessments with limited information, leading them to fall back on emotive tropes of cyberspace that are perpetuated through available channels (Jardine, Porter & Shandler, [Citation 2024](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/19331681.2024.2420669#)).

In this paper, we zoom in on the ingrained public fears surrounding cyberattacks and try to understand how individuals forge such skittish views. We are interested in public opinion since extensive research has now confirmed that ubiquitous cyberattacks manifest grave political consequences. A string of recent experimental studies has demonstrated that cyberattacks arouse severe psychological distress (Backhaus et al., [Citation 2020](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/19331681.2024.2420669#); Canetti et al., [Citation 2017](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/19331681.2024.2420669#); Gross et al., [Citation 2016](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/19331681.2024.2420669#)) which in turn leads to diminished trust in government (Shandler & Gomez, [Citation 2022](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/19331681.2024.2420669#)) heightened support for intrusive government surveillance at the expense of civil liberties (Snider et al., [Citation 2021](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/19331681.2024.2420669#)), expanded support for military retaliation (Kreps & Schneider, [Citation 2019](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/19331681.2024.2420669#); Leal & Musgrave, [Citation 2022](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/19331681.2024.2420669#); Shandler et al., [Citation 2022](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/19331681.2024.2420669#)), and more.

A central mechanism that researchers refer to when explaining public fears of cyberattacks is the _cyber-doom narrative_. This account explains that people view cyber threats through a prism of hyperbole and exaggeration. Through a combination of sensationalized Hollywood depictions (Shires, [Citation 2020](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/19331681.2024.2420669#)) and policymakers drumming up budgets by inflating the cyber threat (Akoto, [Citation 2021](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/19331681.2024.2420669#)), people have formed a perception of cyber power as a near-apocalyptic threat that can bring down planes, blow up power plants, and rain down massive destruction. This causes them to experience intense reactions to cyberattacks – even on par with terrorism and bombings (Shandler, Gross, Backhaus, et al., [Citation 2022](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/19331681.2024.2420669#)).

But how does this exaggerated view of cyber threats coexist with the fact that cyberattacks have failed to live up to the sensationalized and explosive notions that people now expect? Surely once people observe that the attacks are causing minimal damage, their expectations of cyber doom should subside. Yet this is not the case, and successive polls over many years continue to reveal an ingrained public view of cyberattacks as forming a foremost threat to the survival of the nation (Gomez & Whyte, [Citation 2021](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/19331681.2024.2420669#)). In this paper, we raise the possibility that cyber-doom beliefs are not entirely exogenous to cyber threats but are a consequence of how individuals experience cyberattacks. In other words, the beliefs do not come from societal and media forces alone, they stem from the way that people experience, and process cyber-attacks (Kaminska, [Citation 2021](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/19331681.2024.2420669#); Kostyuk & Wayne, [Citation 2021](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/19331681.2024.2420669#)).

We theorize that cyber doom beliefs work in a self-reinforcing cycle. When people experience a cyberattack, they bring along with them a series of underlying beliefs and attitudes. The way that people experience cyberattacks is informed by these beliefs. But the attack also reinforces the beliefs. Put simply, if you believe in cyber doom, then you will experience cyberattacks in a way that reinforces this belief. If you don’t believe in cyber doom, then you will interpret cyberattacks in such a way that they do not pose a major threat. It is important to note that the study’s focus is specifically directed toward exploring the impact of these preexisting perceptions on the post-cyberattack political attitude formation process without delving into the mechanics of the initial genesis of such beliefs. Furthermore, the established perceptions may facilitate the emergence of other forms of bias that reinforce the durability of the cyber doom narrative (e.g., confirmation bias). Thus, the research question to be addressed in this study asks: to what extent do people’s preexisting perceptions of cyber threat impact the way that they form political attitudes following cyberattacks?

To answer this question, we conducted a two-wave field study. To begin, we collected extensive baseline data on threat perceptions and cybersecurity attitudes from a general population sample in Israel, providing a robust set of “pre-treatment” measures. We then waited for a cybersecurity incident to take place in the country, which duly eventuated when a cyberattack struck a major urban hospital. Following the attack, we fielded a follow-up survey reaching 589 returning respondents, at which time we measured their exposure to the cyberattack and tested whether a) exposure to the event shifted threat perception, and b) whether this effect was contingent on preexisting beliefs.

Though we could have chosen to empirically focus our attention on any major cyberattack that came along, we selected to examine a cyberattack targeting the healthcare system. Several factors underlie this choice. Importantly, hospitals are increasingly targeted by cyberattacks. The 2017 WannaCry ransomware incident prominently exposed the vulnerability of the healthcare sector to cyber threats, disrupting vital services throughout the UK health system (Mohurle & Patil, [Citation 2017](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/19331681.2024.2420669#)). In 2020, a German hospital experienced a patient’s death due to a ransomware attack, underlining the life-and-death consequences (Shandler & Gomez, [Citation 2022](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/19331681.2024.2420669#)). Meanwhile, the US experiences hundreds of healthcare related cyberattacks each year, with the 2024 Change Healthcare attack paralyzing hospitals and pharmacies across the country (Abelson & Creswell, [Citation 2024](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/19331681.2024.2420669#)). Hospitals are lucrative targets, housing sensitive patient information in extensively networked systems. The stakes are high, extending beyond financial gains to potential loss of lives, intensifying the urgency to fortify against cyber threats (Lekshmi, [Citation 2022](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/19331681.2024.2420669#)).

The results of our study reinforce the notion that motivated reasoning is key to understanding how people experience cyberattacks. We find that exposure to the hospital cyberattack had no direct effect on threat perception. But when we break down the results by people’s preconceived views of cyber threats – we find a significant moderating effect. Specifically, among those people who already viewed cyberattacks as highly destructive – exposure to the hospital attack amplified their threat perception. For people who thought of cyberattacks as largely innocuous, their perception of cyber threat decreased following exposure to the attack.

This study makes several important contributions to the literature. Theoretically, the study highlights the psychological effects of exposure to cyberattacks on individuals. It recognizes that attitudes and opinions do not form in isolation and that individuals interpret cyberattacks based on their preexisting notions of whether such attacks are threatening or not. Empirically, the study focuses on the impact of cyberattacks on public opinion, particularly within the healthcare sector. However, these findings may also be generalizable to our understanding of cyber-borne threats to critical infrastructure more broadly. Methodologically, this study reaffirms the value of collecting observational data in authentic settings following real-world incidents. As cyberattacks continue to surge globally, these instances provide an opportune platform for conducting contemporaneous research on their impact on civil society.

Cyber doom & exposure to cyberattacks
-------------------------------------

There is now extensive experimental evidence that exposure to even minor cyberattacks generates intense negative emotions such as fear (Kostyuk & Wayne, [Citation 2021](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/19331681.2024.2420669#)), dread (Gomez & Villar, [Citation 2018](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/19331681.2024.2420669#)), anxiety (Backhaus et al., [Citation 2020](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/19331681.2024.2420669#)), and anger (Shandler et al., [Citation 2022](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.1080/19331681.2024.2420669#)). These findings have been consistently replicated across multiple studies and in multiple countries, providing robust evidence for our baseline understanding that cyberattacks arouse a strong emotionally-based reaction.

# Cyber Security Awareness - What is it and why is it important?

Today, cyber security awareness is more crucial than ever. From phishing attacks to malware and ransomware, the threats to our online security are constantly evolving. It is important to understand these common cyber security threats in order to protect ourselves effectively.

By using strong passwords, keeping software updated, and being cautious of suspicious emails, we can defend against cyber attacks. Educating ourselves on cyber security best practices and staying informed about current threats are also key steps in improving our cyber security awareness.

On this page, we'll cover:

* [What is cyber security awareness?](#what)
* [Why Is cyber security awareness important?](#why)
* [What are the common cyber security threats?](#common)
* [How can you protect yourself from cyber attacks?](#how)
* [How can you improve your cyber security awareness?](#you)
* [Frequently Asked Questions](#faq)

Key takeaways:

* Cyber security awareness is crucial in protecting yourself from various cyber threats.
* Common cyber threats include phishing attacks, malware, social engineering, insider threats, and DoS attacks.
* To improve your cyber security awareness, stay informed, educate yourself, participate in training, and share your knowledge with others.

## 

## 

## What is cyber security awareness?

[Cyber Security](/cyber-security/) Awareness refers to the knowledge and practices that individuals within organisations possess to protect sensitive information and systems from cyber threats.

By understanding the risks associated with cyber threats, employees can play a crucial role in safeguarding their organisation's assets. Training programmes focus on equipping staff with the necessary skills to identify and respond to potential security breaches.

An informed workforce is the first line of defence against cyberattacks. For instance, phishing attacks, where employees unknowingly click on malicious links or provide sensitive information, can lead to significant data breaches.

## 

## Why Is cyber security awareness important?

Cyber Security Awareness is crucial for organisations to prevent data breaches, phishing scams, and security incidents by enhancing their cyber security posture.

In the digital age, where technology plays a central role in everyday operations, the importance of cyber security awareness cannot be overstated. With cyber threats becoming increasingly sophisticated, organisations need to arm their employees with the knowledge and skills to detect and prevent potential attacks.

By conducting regular awareness training sessions, businesses can enable their staff to identify suspicious emails, links, or messages that could potentially lead to devastating consequences. This proactive approach not only safeguards sensitive data but also contributes to building a strong and resilient cyber defence strategy.

## 

## What are the common cyber security threats?

Common [cyber security threats](/cyber-security/threats/) include phishing scams, malware, ransomware, social engineering tactics, insider threats, and denial of service attacks.

Phishing scams are one of the most prevalent cyber threats, where hackers masquerade as legitimate entities to deceive individuals into giving out sensitive information such as passwords or credit card details. An example of this is the 2016 phishing attack targeting Gmail users in which fake Google Docs links were used to steal login credentials.

Malware, another common threat, refers to malicious software designed to cause harm, such as viruses, worms, or spyware. For instance, the WannaCry ransomware attack in 2017 infected over 200,000 systems worldwide, encrypting data until a ransom was paid.

### Phishing attacks

Phishing Attacks are misleading attempts to acquire sensitive information such as passwords or financial data through fraudulent emails or websites.

These attacks have become increasingly widespread in today's digital world, posing significant risks to individuals and organisations alike. Email security solutions play a crucial role in protecting against such threats by implementing encryption, junk mail filters, and multi-factor authentication.

One must also emphasise phishing awareness training as a proactive measure to enable users to identify and stop phishing attempts effectively. By educating individuals on common phishing tactics and fostering a culture of vigilance, organisations can establish a strong defence against these malicious schemes.

### Malware and ransomware

Malware and Ransomware are malicious software programmes designed to infiltrate systems, encrypt files, and extort money from victims.

These cyber threats pose significant risks to individuals, businesses, and even governments worldwide. With the advancements in technology, cybercriminals have become more sophisticated in their tactics, making it increasingly challenging to detect and mitigate such attacks. Fortunately, technology also plays a vital role in defending against these threats by continuously evolving and improving security measures.

Implementing strong security protocols and regularly updating antivirus software are crucial steps in safeguarding against malware and ransomware. It is essential for organisations and individuals alike to prioritise cybersecurity awareness and adopt robust security practices to minimise the likelihood of falling victim to these cyber threats.

### Social engineering

Social Engineering involves manipulating individuals to divulge confidential information or perform actions that compromise security, often exploiting human error.

These attacks can take various forms, such as phishing emails, pretexting, or baiting, all aiming to deceive individuals into disclosing sensitive data or clicking on malicious links. Attackers often exploit psychological principles or use convincing social interactions to gain the trust of their targets. The success of social engineering relies heavily on exploiting human emotions, curiosity, or trust, making it imperative for individuals to stay vigilant and sceptical in their online interactions.

By fostering a culture of scepticism and promoting security awareness training, organisations can enable their employees to recognise and thwart social engineering attempts effectively.

### Insider Threats

Insider Threats refer to risks posed by individuals within an organisation who misuse their access privileges, making policy acknowledgement and security awareness services crucial.

These threats can come from employees, contractors, or even former staff members who still have access rights. The dangers of insider threats include data breaches, intellectual property theft, and sabotage. Organisations must implement comprehensive policies that address the potential risks posed by insiders.

By establishing clear guidelines and restrictions on access to sensitive information, companies can minimise the likelihood of insider attacks. Providing security awareness services to educate employees on identifying and reporting suspicious behaviour is essential in creating a culture of vigilance within the organisation.

### Denial of Service (DoS) attacks

Denial of Service (DoS) Attacks overwhelm a system or network with excessive traffic, emphasizing the importance of vulnerability assessments and penetration tests.

These attacks disrupt services by flooding a network with traffic, making it unavailable to legitimate users. Vulnerability assessments play a crucial role in identifying weak points that malicious actors can exploit, aiding organizations in strengthening their defenses proactively.

Carrying out penetration tests allows companies to replicate real-world attacks and evaluate the resilience of their systems, offering valuable insights into potential vulnerabilities and avenues for improvement. Through the implementation of these security measures, businesses can enhance their protection against the harmful effects of DoS attacks.

## How can you protect yourself from cyber attacks?

Protecting yourself from cyber attacks involves following best practices, maintaining GDPR compliance, and participating in a robust security awareness programme.

Implementing regular software updates and patches across all devices is crucial in preventing vulnerabilities that can be exploited by cyber attackers. Encrypting sensitive data at rest and in transit adds an extra layer of protection to your information. It is important to limit access to data on a need-to-know basis, implementing strong password policies, and enabling multi-factor authentication to strengthen your defenses against unauthorized access.

### Use strong and unique passwords

Using strong and unique passwords is essential to safeguarding your accounts and mitigating cyber threats, as recommended by IT Governance.

In today's digital age, where cyber attacks are becoming increasingly sophisticated, a robust password is your first line of defence against unauthorised access to personal information and sensitive data. Creating a complex password that includes a combination of letters, numbers, and special characters is crucial to enhance security levels.

It is highly advisable to use different passwords for each online account to prevent a domino effect in case one password is compromised. Remembering multiple complex passwords can be challenging, which is where password management tools come into play.

### Keep your software and devices updated

Regularly updating your software and devices is crucial to patching vulnerabilities and fortifying defenses against evolving cyber attacks.

In today's digital age, where hackers are constantly finding new ways to exploit weaknesses in software, staying on top of updates is paramount. By ensuring that your systems are equipped with the latest security patches, you are effectively closing doors that cybercriminals might otherwise infiltrate.

Timely updates serve as a proactive measure to prevent potential breaches and data theft, acting as a proactive shield against diverse cyber threats. These updates not only enhance the overall performance of your devices but also address any known security gaps that could be exploited by malicious actors.

### Be cautious of suspicious emails and links

Being cautious of suspicious emails and links is essential, especially when working remotely, to avoid falling victim to phishing scams and other information security threats.

Phishing emails are a significant risk in the cyber world. They are crafted to deceive individuals into revealing sensitive information or executing harmful actions. With the rise of remote working, the vulnerability to such attacks has increased. Therefore, it is crucial to remain vigilant and educate yourself on the red flags of phishing emails.

One key practice is to verify the sender's identity before clicking on any links or attachments. Remember, legitimate organisations will never ask for sensitive information via email. Take the time to hover over links to check the destination URL and beware of urgent language or requests for immediate action.

### Use a Virtual Private Network (VPN)

Utilising a Virtual Private Network (VPN) enhances your cyber security by encrypting data transmissions and aligning with robust cyber security policies and fully managed services.

When you use a VPN, all your online activities are shielded from prying eyes, including sensitive data transfers such as financial transactions and personal information exchanges. This encrypted tunnel created by VPN technology acts as a protective barrier against potential cyber threats lurking on public networks.

Plus encryption, VPNs play a crucial role in [ensuring compliance with cyber security](/cyber-security/compliance/) policies established by organisations and regulatory bodies. By adhering to these guidelines, you not only safeguard your own data but also contribute to a safer online environment for all users.

Opting for fully managed VPN services offers an extra layer of security through continuous monitoring, threat detection, and proactive measures to thwart cyber attacks. With managed services, experts handle the technical aspects, allowing you to focus on your online activities without worrying about potential vulnerabilities.

### Enable two-factor authentication

Enabling Two-Factor Authentication provides an extra layer of security for accounts, employing technology-driven safeguards to prevent unauthorized access.

Two-Factor Authentication offers several advantages in enhancing account security. It requires users to provide two pieces of identification, typically something they know (like a password) and something they have (like a smartphone). This dual verification process greatly reduces the risk of unauthorized access, as even if one factor is compromised, the account remains secure. 2FA adds complexity for potential hackers, making it more challenging for them to breach an account.

Implementing and managing 2FA is relatively simple. Many online services and applications offer the option to enable this feature in their account security settings. Users should ensure they choose strong, unique passwords for each account and link a trusted device for the second authentication factor. Regularly updating passwords and reviewing authorized devices can also help maintain the security of 2FA.

This security measure aligns well with technological advancements by leveraging biometrics, such as fingerprint or facial recognition, as the second factor of authentication. As our reliance on digital platforms continues to grow, integrating 2FA is a proactive step towards protecting sensitive information from potential cyber threats and data breaches.

## How can you improve your cyber security awareness?

Enhancing your Cyber Security Awareness involves adopting best practices, engaging in a security awareness programme, and leveraging resources such as those provided by IT Governance.

One effective strategy to boost your cyber security awareness is to stay updated on the latest threats and vulnerabilities. This can be achieved by subscribing to credible cybersecurity news sources and following industry experts on social media. By regularly educating yourself on emerging risks, you can proactively strengthen your defenses.

Additionally, active participation in security awareness programmes is crucial for reinforcing good security habits. Encourage your peers and colleagues to join in these initiatives, as collective awareness and vigilance can significantly enhance overall cyber resilience.

IT Governance offers a wealth of resources to support your ongoing journey towards improved cyber security. From informative guides to interactive training modules, these tools can enable you with the knowledge and skills needed to protect yourself and your organization from potential cyber threats.

### Stay informed about current cyber threats

Staying informed about current cyber threats is vital to proactively defend against emerging risks and cyber attacks in the rapidly evolving digital landscape.

Keeping abreast of the latest cyber threat intelligence not only enables individuals and organisations to anticipate potential vulnerabilities but also enables them to implement robust security measures to mitigate any potential risks.

By monitoring reputable cybersecurity news outlets, threat intelligence platforms, and government alerts, one can gain valuable insights into existing and emerging cyber threats. Cybercriminals continuously adapt and evolve their tactics, making regular updates on threat intelligence critical for maintaining a resilient cybersecurity posture.

### Educate yourself on cyber security best practices

Educating yourself on Cyber Security Best Practices equips you with the knowledge and skills to navigate security challenges effectively, complementing awareness courses and cyber security policies.

By staying informed about the latest cyber threats and preventive measures, you can enhance your digital defence mechanisms. Understanding common attack vectors, such as phishing scams and ransomware, allows you to recognise and mitigate potential risks proactively.

Enrolling in cyber security awareness courses provides hands-on training in identifying vulnerabilities and implementing protective measures. These courses often cover topics like data encryption, network security, and incident response, enhancing your overall cyber hygiene.

Adhering to cyber security policies within your organisation establishes a culture of collective responsibility and accountability. Consistent implementation of these policies fosters a secure work environment and protects sensitive data from unauthorised access or breaches.

### Participate in cyber security training programmes

Engaging in Cyber Security Training Programmes enhances your ability to recognise and respond to security threats effectively, aligning with GDPR legislation requirements for staff training.

By participating in cyber security training programmes, individuals across various industries can gain the crucial knowledge and skills needed to safeguard sensitive information and prevent cyber attacks.

For those in the healthcare sector, specialised training courses such as Certified Healthcare Information Systems Security Practitioner (CHISSP) can provide comprehensive insights into securing patient data and complying with HIPAA regulations.

Likewise, professionals in the finance industry may benefit from courses like Certified Information Systems Auditor (CISA) to strengthen their understanding of risk management and compliance with financial regulations.

### Share your knowledge with others

Sharing your Cyber Security knowledge with others fosters a culture of security awareness and behavioural change, in line with ISO 27001 standards for information security management.

When team members are equipped with the necessary skills and understanding of potential cyber security threats, they are better prepared to identify and respond to any suspicious activities. By encouraging open discussions and knowledge-sharing sessions, employees can become more vigilant in spotting phishing attempts, malware threats, and [other cyber risks](/cyber-security/risks/). This collective effort not only strengthens the organisation's defence mechanisms but also cultivates a proactive security mindset among staff members, ultimately contributing to a more secure digital environment.

## Frequently Asked Questions

### What is cyber security awareness?

Cyber Security Awareness refers to the knowledge, skills, and attitudes necessary to protect sensitive information and computer systems from cyber attacks.

### Why is cyber security awareness important?

Cyber attacks are becoming more frequent and sophisticated, making it crucial for individuals and organizations to be aware of potential threats and how to protect themselves.

### How can I improve my cyber security awareness?

You can improve your Cyber Security Awareness by staying informed about current threats and best practices, using strong and unique passwords, and being cautious of suspicious emails or requests for personal information.

### What are some common cyber threats?

Cyber threats include phishing, malware, ransomware, social engineering, and identity theft.

### How can I protect my personal information online?

To protect your personal information online, you should regularly update your devices and software, avoid using public Wi-Fi for sensitive activities, and use multi-factor authentication whenever possible.

### What should I do if I suspect I've been a victim of a cyber attack?

If you believe you have been a victim of a cyber attack, you should immediately change your passwords, contact your financial institutions, and report the incident to the proper authorities.
